variables.content: Let $m^{*}$ denote the quasiparticle effective mass at the Fermi level of a Fermi-liquid state, defined operationally via the low-temperature specific heat coefficient $\gamma$ as $\gamma\propto m^{*}$ (so $\gamma=C/T$ as $T\to0$), and let $T_{\rm coh}$ denote the temperature below which Fermi-liquid thermodynamics (linear specific heat and temperature-independent susceptibilities) are observed. The proposition is that, in a conventional Fermi liquid of itinerant quasiparticles, $T_{\rm coh}$ is proportional to an inverse effective-mass scale, i.e., $T_{\rm coh}\propto 1/m^{*}$ up to factors set by band parameters and quasiparticle residue, which is the standard phenomenological relation between effective mass and the energy window of coherent quasiparticle behavior.

Consider a three-dimensional interacting SU($N_{c}$) Fermi gas at low temperature where Landau Fermi-liquid phenomenology applies. Let $M$ be the bare particle mass, $M^{*}$ the quasiparticle effective mass on the Fermi surface, and $k_{\mu}\equiv\sqrt{2M\mu}$. The proposition is: to second order in the small parameter $k_{\mu}a$, the quasiparticle effective mass is given by
      $
      \displaystyle \frac{M^{*}}{M}=1+\left(N_{c}-1\right)\frac{8}{15\pi^{2}}(7\ln 2-1)\left(k_{\mu}a\right)^{2},
      $
      provided that the system is in the Fermi-liquid regime (no phase transitions or strong-coupling breakdown) and that vertex corrections beyond the order shown are negligible in the weak-coupling limit.
      [32]

For a homogeneous three-dimensional electron Fermi liquid with bare electron mass $M$, Landau-quasiparticle effective mass $M^{*}$, Fermi momentum $p_{\mathrm{F}}$, and noninteracting density of states at the Fermi level $N_{0}$, and with $F^{1}(p_{\mathrm{F}},p_{\mathrm{F}})$ denoting the $p$-wave ($\ell=1$) component of the Landau interaction amplitude evaluated at equal incoming and outgoing momenta $p_{\mathrm{F}}$, the quasiparticle effective mass satisfies the Landau–Fermi–liquid relation
      $$
      \frac{M^{*}}{M}=\frac{1}{1-\dfrac{N_{0}F^{1}(p_{\mathrm{F}},p_{\mathrm{F}})}{3}}.
      $$
      [8]

The quasiparticle effective-mass ratio for a Fermi liquid can be written in terms of derivatives of the real part of the retarded self-energy evaluated on the Fermi surface (momentum p = p0 and energy ε = 0) as
      m*/m = [1 − (∂/∂ε) Re Σ^R(p0,ε)|_{ε=0}] / [1 + (m/p0) (∂/∂p) Re Σ^R(p,ε)|_{p=p0,ε=0}],
      where p0 denotes the Fermi momentum, m is the bare mass, m* is the quasiparticle effective mass, and Re Σ^R is the real part of the retarded single-particle self-energy.

The kinetic-energy renormalization factor q obtained from the Gutzwiller variational state is interpreted in the Fermi-liquid sense via the relation 1/q = m^{*}/m, where m^{*} denotes the Fermi-liquid quasiparticle effective mass and m denotes the bare electron mass; this identification is used to translate reductions in q into quasiparticle mass enhancement.

Assuming the standard Landau Fermi-liquid relation that the low-temperature linear specific-heat coefficient \gamma is proportional to the quasiparticle density of states at the Fermi surface (hence proportional to the quasiparticle effective mass m^{*}), the measured value \gamma=2.11\ \mathrm{K}^{-1} implies an effective-mass ratio m^{*}/m\approx 2.12 (where m is the bare fermion mass) and, under the usual formulas that relate the effective mass renormalization to the first Landau Fermi-liquid parameter, yields F_{1}\approx 3.36.

Assume standard Fermi-liquid relations linking the low-energy derivative of the real self-energy to the quasiparticle residue and effective mass as follows: define $\lambda\equiv -\left.\frac{d\Sigma'(\varepsilon)}{d\varepsilon}\right|_{\varepsilon=0}$, define the quasiparticle residue $Z\equiv 1/(1+\lambda)$, define the band mass $m_{b}$ as the curvature-derived effective mass of the non-interacting DFT band at the Fermi momentum, and assume that the interacting quasiparticle effective mass satisfies $m^{*}/m_{b}=Z^{-1}$, with the additional assumptions that the electronic self-energy is approximately momentum-independent near the Fermi surface (local approximation) and that Fermi-liquid quasiparticle formulas apply in the low-energy regime considered.

The simultaneous observation of a large Fermi-surface cross-sectional area (the 6.03 kT belly orbit) and a strongly enhanced quasiparticle effective mass m* = 6(2) m_e in NiS₂ pressurized to ≈3.8 GPa is consistent with the Brinkman–Rice picture of a correlated Fermi liquid near a Mott insulator, in which coherent Fermi-liquid quasiparticles form a heavy Fermi liquid whose Fermi-surface volume remains equal to that of the corresponding uncorrelated band while the quasiparticle spectral weight Z (the residue of the single-particle Green's function at the quasiparticle pole) and the quasiparticle velocities are strongly reduced.

For a homogeneous, isotropic, three-dimensional Fermi liquid with a single-band dispersion near the Fermi surface, the quasiparticle density of states per spin at the Fermi level is given by the standard Landau-Fermi-liquid expression
      $$
      N(E_{F})=\frac{M^{*}p_{F}}{\pi^{2}},
      $$
      where M^{*} is the Landau quasiparticle effective mass (the mass parameter entering the low-energy dispersion near the Fermi momentum), p_{F} is the magnitude of the Fermi momentum, and N(E_{F}) denotes the single-particle density of states at the chemical potential; this expression neglects band-structure anisotropies and multi-band contributions.

In the LLL computation of the anomalous quasiparticle momentum density, the calculation performed neglects Fermi-liquid interaction corrections to the quasiparticle spectrum and to quasiparticle momentum flow (i.e., sets the quasiparticle effective-mass corrections m^*/m=1 and ignores backflow renormalizations). The proposition is that, for the leading distinction between the T=0 vacuum anomalous current and the universal near-node T^{2} thermal quasiparticle contribution, neglecting these Fermi-liquid corrections yields an accurate leading-order expression for the thermal piece; the residual corrections due to Fermi-liquid physics are treated separately in hydrodynamic/Fermi-liquid formulas.
      [16]
